label: SES-067 PI-029 slice B build planning complete: 4 decisions settled (DEC-211 derived endpoint scope option B — ship only /conversations/{id}/commits, defer /workstreams/{id}/commits; DEC-212 PATCH updatability — commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, and commit_conversation_id are updatable, identity pair commit_identifier and commit_sha are not; DEC-213 by-sha endpoint specifics — minimum prefix length 4, uppercase input lowercased before query, ambiguous-prefix 409 response uses 'candidates' field with full 40-char SHAs; DEC-214 list endpoint sort and order parameters — default sort commit_committed_at descending matching the UI master pane, sortable column set locked to a known-safe subset). Slice B Claude Code prompt authored and queued for execution. 1 session, 4 decisions (DEC-211..214), 5 references (4 decided_in + 1 is_about session→PI-029).

session.title: PI-029 slice B build planning — derived endpoint scope, PATCH updatability scope, by-sha endpoint specifics, list sort/order parameters; Claude Code prompt for commit access layer + REST endpoints + tests authored
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai ARCHITECTURE-mode planning conversation. Opened against the PI-029 slice B kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-029-slice-b-commit-access-layer-and-rest-endpoints-kickoff.md to scope the access-layer repository, REST endpoints, Pydantic schemas, router registration, and test coverage for the seventh governance entity type (commit), building on slice A's commit 9c1d3b7 which landed migration 0012, the Commit ORM model, and v0.8 vocab. The conversation settled five build-planning questions named in the kickoff (Q1 derived endpoint scope, Q2 by-sha endpoint specifics with three sub-questions, Q3 commit_parent_shas updatability, Q4 commit_files_changed_count updatability, Q5 list sort parameters) plus surfaced one path-correction issue (the kickoff cited access/commit.py and api/commits.py as target paths, but the actual repo convention is access/repositories/commits.py and api/routers/commits.py, parallel to every existing governance entity type). Under operating-mode discipline, Q1 was the single stop-the-flow consequential decision (real downstream impact AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes — ship one derived endpoint, both, or neither); Q2/Q3/Q4/Q5 were decide-and-announce, folded into the slice B prompt with rationale captured here as DEC-212, DEC-213, and DEC-214 since each settles a meaningful question the spec deferred to build-planning. The four decisions are committed alongside this close-out and apply ahead of the slice B Claude Code execution.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Project default operating mode: ARCHITECTURE.\n\nUse PROTOTYPE mode when explicitly declared in a kickoff prompt for early exploratory work — first-cut schemas, draft methodologies, paste-and-iterate exploration where speed of testing matters more than completeness.\n\nUse DETAIL mode when explicitly declared for code writing in the crmbuilder repo, Claude Code prompt authoring, or surgical edits to existing files..." attached to the conversation's opening turn alongside the PI-029 slice B kickoff path (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-029-slice-b-commit-access-layer-and-rest-endpoints-kickoff.md).

Pre-flight verification. Pulled the crmbuilder repo at HEAD 8bf65b1 (the PI-029 slice B kickoff commit itself, with no intervening commits), read CLAUDE.md including the v0.7 governance entity release section and the working conventions push convention. Read commit.md v1.0 fully — §3.2 fields and validation, §3.3 relationships including the DEC-199 FK-over-references-edge deviation, §3.4 status-free documentary lifecycle with mis-ingestion correction posture and soft-delete semantics, §3.5 endpoint set with the new by-sha four-case behavior, §3.6 UI scope (PI-031, out of scope for slice B), §3.7 acceptance criteria 4-11 (slice B's coverage gate), §3.8 open questions for build-planning. Read slice A's commit 9c1d3b7 commit message to verify what landed (migration 0012, Commit ORM model lines 918-1002 in models.py, vocab.py v0.8 updates, 21 new tests, migration applied to CRMBUILDER engagement DB). Read the work_tickets repository pattern at access/repositories/work_tickets.py (447 lines) as the closest CRUD orchestration parallel, the work_tickets router at api/routers/work_tickets.py (122 lines), the _governance helpers at access/repositories/_governance.py (the shared field validators and edge helpers), the Pydantic schema patterns at api/schemas.py around the WorkTicketCreateIn cluster, and the work_ticket access-layer test patterns at tests/crmbuilder_v2/access/test_work_ticket.py. Confirmed the references API write contract uses the field key 'relationship' (per GovernanceEdgeIn schema and access/repositories/references.py line 68 to_dict rename of relationship_kind → relationship); the SES-066 apply prompt's spot-check curl snippet line 133 uses 'relationship_kind' but reads from the API response body where the key is 'relationship' — a latent typo in that snippet that does not block the apply, just produces a less-useful spot-check output. Not in scope to fix here.

Path correction surfaced. The kickoff cited two target paths: access/commit.py and api/commits.py. The repo convention verified by inspecting every existing governance entity module is access/repositories/<entity>.py (under repositories/, file name plural to match the table) and api/routers/<entity>.py (under routers/). Slice B prompt uses the correct paths regardless; the kickoff typo is noted in the prompt's Net Effect block so any future cross-reference is unambiguous.

Decision 1 — derived endpoint scope (DEC-211). The kickoff's Q1 named two candidate derived endpoints from commit.md §3.8.1: GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits (one-hop FK lookup) and GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits (two-hop traversal through conversations). The eight-element decision template was applied — concrete example used SES-063 (the PI-028 commit-schema-spec conversation that produced the spec commit and the slice A merge commit 9c1d3b7), then the workstream-level example for the Code Change Lifecycle workstream that PI-029 establishes. Three options named with concrete behavior: A ship both, B ship only the one-hop, C ship neither. The recommendation was B with one-line rationale that frequency-of-traversal justifies the one-hop derived endpoint by the same logic DEC-199 used to justify the FK column, while the two-hop workstream endpoint is speculative without operational evidence — adding it now repeats the pattern DEC-133 was written to discourage. Honest costs of B documented: one extra route handler plus three tests, asymmetry across governance entities until or unless future build-planning conversations add parallels (work_tickets, close_out_payloads currently have no conversation-scoped derived endpoint), client-side fan-out cost for workstream-scoped queries until PI-032+ adds the two-hop endpoint. Approved. The derived endpoint is mounted on the conversations router rather than the commits router so the URL path nests naturally under /conversations.

Decision 2 — PATCH updatability scope (DEC-212). The kickoff's Q3 surfaced an ambiguity in commit.md §3.4.3 about whether commit_parent_shas is identity-like (in which case PATCH should reject changes) or correctable metadata (in which case PATCH should admit changes). The spec's §3.4.3 language admits PATCH for 'any correction that doesn't change the commit's identity' but does not explicitly enumerate which fields qualify as identity. Q4 was a parallel question about commit_files_changed_count. Settled: the identity-fields-only restriction stays on commit_identifier and commit_sha; every other field including commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, and commit_conversation_id is updatable via PATCH for the administrative-correction path. Rationale: the rare-correction case the spec's PATCH path was designed for is exactly the mis-ingestion scenario where the helper script (PI-030 work) captures the wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P or the wrong file-change count from git diff-tree, or the wrong conversation attribution per methodology §5.6's between-conversations-manual-commit concern. Narrowing PATCH to a smaller field set would force soft-delete-and-recreate for those scenarios, which §3.4.3 explicitly characterizes as 'reserved for the case where the correction changes the entity identity' — and parent SHAs, file counts, and conversation attribution are NOT identity. The DB-level constraint that does protect identity (UNIQUE on commit_sha) holds regardless.

Decision 3 — by-sha endpoint specifics (DEC-213). The kickoff's Q2 named three sub-questions: (a) minimum prefix length; (b) case normalization; (c) ambiguous-prefix 409 response body shape. Settled together as one decision since they are different facets of the same endpoint contract. (a) Minimum prefix length 4 hex characters per spec §3.5.1's '4+' language; prefixes of length 1-3 return HTTP 422 with code prefix_too_short. Git's --short default is 7 but the spec settled on 4 and the conversation did not surface evidence to bump it. (b) Uppercase or mixed-case input is lowercased before the query rather than rejected with HTTP 422; the schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1, and rejecting uppercase would be a worse API ergonomic than transparently normalizing. (c) Ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses the field name 'candidates' (per the spec's sketch) carrying full 40-char SHAs only (not identifiers, not both); the caller already has SHAs as the natural key, so returning the V2 identifiers in the candidate list would invite confusion about which is canonical.

Decision 4 — list endpoint sort and order parameters (DEC-214). The kickoff's Q5 asked whether the list endpoint's default sort and the sort/order query parameter shape should match the V2 list-endpoint pattern. Settled: API default is commit_committed_at descending matching the UI master pane convention from commit.md §3.6.2 and parallel to deposit_event's deviation from V2's default ascending sort per the audit-log nature of the entity. The endpoint accepts ?sort=<column> and ?order=asc|desc per V2's list-endpoint pattern; the sortable column set is locked at the access layer to a known-safe subset (commit_identifier, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at) to prevent free-form column injection via the sort parameter. Invalid sort columns return HTTP 422 with code invalid_sort_column; invalid order values return HTTP 422 with code invalid_order.

Deliverable authored. PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-B-commit-access-layer-and-rest.md — 2062-line Claude Code prompt with full code skeletons for the new repository module (commits.py), the new REST router (commits.py at api/routers/), the new test files (test_commit.py for access layer, test_commit_api.py for REST endpoints), the surgical edits to _helpers.py (next_prefixed_identifier gains a width keyword default 3 for backward compatibility), schemas.py (three new Pydantic classes), main.py (router registration), and conversations.py (the derived endpoint per DEC-211). The prompt's Pre-flight section includes a check that the running crmbuilder-v2-api process has been restarted to pick up the v0.8 vocab from slice A — slice A's commit message flagged that PID 3226223 (or whatever the current PID is) holds pre-v0.8 vocab in memory and must be restarted before tests can validate end-to-end. Test count target documented as 35+ new passing across the two new files, with the post-slice expectation of >= 1425 total v2 suite passing (up from slice A's 1391 baseline).
session.artifacts_produced: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_067.json (this payload — bundles 1 session record, 4 decisions DEC-211..214, 5 references: 4 decided_in + 1 is_about session→PI-029); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-067.md (apply prompt for this payload); PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-pi-029-B-commit-access-layer-and-rest.md (slice B Claude Code prompt — installs access-layer repository, REST router, Pydantic schemas, router registration, derived endpoint on conversations router, and full test coverage for acceptance criteria 4-11 plus DEC-211..214 specifics; 2062 lines including code skeletons).
session.in_flight_at_end:
Slice B Claude Code prompt queued for execution. Apply order: this close-out (SES-067) first to land DEC-211..214 plus the supporting decided_in and is_about references; then Doug runs the slice B prompt via Claude Code at his terminal to land the new repository, router, schemas, and tests. The slice B prompt's Done block asks Claude Code to report the pre-slice test baseline (1391 passed + 3 skipped per slice A's commit message, or whatever it is at the time the prompt runs), the post-slice counts, the commit SHA, and any deviations from the prompt with rationale.

After slice B lands, the remaining PI-029-adjacent work splits across separate planning items:
- PI-030: apply_close_out.py integration that wires the new commits close-out payload section. The payload schema must be extended to admit a top-level commits array; the apply script then POSTs each entry through the new POST /commits endpoint in the same fixed apply order pattern (sessions → decisions → planning_items → references → commits → deposit_event lazy-create). The script also needs to compute parent_shas and files_changed_count per commit from git log/diff-tree, and to attribute each commit to its producing conversation (the close-out's session block's CONV parent). Kickoff for PI-030 is the natural next conversation.
- PI-031: Commits panel UI per commit.md §3.6 — sidebar entry in the Governance group, master pane with the columns and filters specified, detail pane with the sections specified, create/edit/delete dialogs for the rare administrative-correction case.
- PI-033: historical commit back-fill — the one-time script that walks the git histories of crmbuilder and ClevelandBusinessMentoring repos, attributes each commit to its producing conversation per the close-out-time-window heuristic, and POSTs them through the new endpoints.

No new planning items surfaced by SES-067. PI-029 stays Open; it closes when slice B and its Claude Code execution complete (PI-030, PI-031, PI-033 are PI-029-adjacent successors, not PI-029 itself).

Kickoff for PI-030 will be authored at slice B close (after Claude Code reports the slice B execution result), following the kickoff-chain rule from PI-022/PI-023/PI-024/PI-025/PI-026. The PI-030 kickoff inherits the close-out payload schema decision (commits section bundled into the standard payload, not authored separately) and the apply-script field-extraction conventions from this conversation's slice B prompt.

decisions.title: PI-029 slice B ships only the one-hop derived endpoint GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits; the two-hop GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits variant is deferred
decisions.context: commit.md §3.8.1 named two candidate derived endpoints and deferred the URL-shape decision to build-planning: (a) GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits — list every commit produced by a specific conversation, equivalent to GET /commits?commit_conversation_id=<id> against the standard list endpoint but more discoverable as a nested URL; (b) GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits — list every commit produced by any conversation belonging to the workstream, a two-hop query (workstream → conversations → commits) implementable either as a two-step query at the endpoint or via denormalization of workstream linkage onto the commits table. The kickoff's Q1 surfaced this as a real-downstream-impact AND two-viable-options stop-the-flow decision under operating-mode discipline. Methodology §6 audit queries walk commit → conversation routinely; conversation → commits is the reverse walk and equally common at audit time, justifying frequency-of-traversal arguments parallel to DEC-199's FK-column justification. The workstream → commits walk is two-hop, lower-frequency, and no operational audit query has yet been documented at that scope. Cross-entity consistency cuts both ways: the existing six v0.7 governance entities (workstream, conversation, reference_book, work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) do NOT have FK-scoped derived endpoints — they expose ?<fk>= filters on their list endpoints and stop there. Shipping /conversations/{id}/commits makes commit the first governance entity with a parent-scoped derived URL, setting a precedent future workstreams will be asked to match for their own dense FK relationships.
decisions.decision: Ship Option B — only the one-hop derived endpoint GET /conversations/{conversation_identifier}/commits in PI-029 slice B. Defer the two-hop GET /workstreams/{workstream_identifier}/commits endpoint pending operational evidence that the workstream-scoped query pattern is worth the two-hop machinery or the workstream-denormalization migration. The endpoint accepts standard list-endpoint query parameters (?include_deleted, ?commit_repository, ?sort, ?order, ?limit, ?offset) but locks commit_conversation_id to the path identifier (rejecting any conflicting ?commit_conversation_id= query parameter). The endpoint returns HTTP 404 with code conversation_not_found if the named conversation does not exist (existence checked against soft-deleted conversations too so that commits attributed to a restored conversation can still be listed during administrative correction); returns HTTP 200 with empty array if the conversation exists but produced zero commits. The endpoint is mounted on the conversations router rather than the commits router so the URL path nests naturally under /conversations.
decisions.rationale: Frequency-of-traversal justifies the one-hop derived endpoint by the same logic DEC-199 used to justify the FK column (dense entity type, every audit query walks the FK direction, the discoverable URL pays itself back through use). The two-hop workstream endpoint is speculative for v0.8 — no operational audit query has yet been documented at that scope, and adding it now without evidence repeats the pattern DEC-133 was written to discourage (do not introduce typed structure absent frequency justification). The honest cost of asymmetry across governance entities (work_tickets, close_out_payloads do not have parallel conversation-scoped derived endpoints) is acknowledged and accepted; future build-planning conversations may add the parallels if frequency justifies, or may accept the asymmetry permanently. The client-side fan-out cost for workstream-scoped queries (two round trips: one to list the workstream's conversations, one to filter commits by that list) is acceptable for v0.8's expected commit volume of hundreds, not tens of thousands.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option A: ship both /conversations/{id}/commits and /workstreams/{id}/commits. Rejected — the workstream endpoint is speculative for v0.8 and adds two-hop query machinery (or a denormalization migration) for a use case that has not yet appeared operationally. Adding it now is more machinery than the evidence supports. If the workstream-scoped use case emerges, PI-032+ adds it as one additive migration plus one route handler. Option C: ship neither; rely on GET /commits?commit_conversation_id=<id> against the bare list endpoint. Rejected — the FK column was added per DEC-199 specifically because the conversation → commits walk is high-frequency; the discoverable URL is the natural API-surface complement to that storage decision, and shipping the access without the discoverable URL undersells the storage investment. The two options match the spec's §3.8.1 enumeration; no third candidate URL shape was viable.
decisions.consequences: Slice B's REST surface includes one new derived endpoint (mounted on the conversations router) plus its three tests (happy path, empty list, 404). The /commits routes themselves carry the bare list endpoint with the ?commit_conversation_id= filter as the underlying mechanism that the derived endpoint delegates to. Cross-entity asymmetry persists until or unless future build-planning conversations add parallel conversation-scoped derived endpoints for work_tickets, close_out_payloads, etc. — the asymmetry is a real signal to API consumers that commit is treated differently because of its FK column and frequency profile. PI-032+ is the natural slot for adding /workstreams/{id}/commits if operational evidence emerges; the deferred-to-build-planning entry in §3.8.1 records the pending decision.

decisions.title: Every commit field except the identity pair (commit_identifier, commit_sha) is updatable via PATCH for the administrative-correction path
decisions.context: commit.md §3.4.3 admits PATCH for 'any correction that doesn't change the commit's identity' but does not explicitly enumerate which fields qualify as identity beyond stating that commit_identifier and commit_sha are not updatable. The kickoff's Q3 surfaced commit_parent_shas as an ambiguous case: arguably part of identity (they define the commit's position in the git DAG) but also a candidate for the rare-correction case (mis-ingestion via apply-script bug capturing wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P). The kickoff's Q4 raised the parallel question for commit_files_changed_count and other denormalized fields. The decision determines the patchable field set the access-layer's _PATCHABLE_FIELDS frozenset enforces and the corresponding PatchIn Pydantic schema's optional field list.
decisions.decision: The identity-fields-only PATCH restriction stays on commit_identifier and commit_sha. Every other field is updatable via PATCH: commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, and commit_conversation_id. commit_conversation_id updates trigger the FK existence check (the new conversation must exist, even as a soft-deleted record). Attempts to patch commit_identifier or commit_sha return HTTP 422 with field_not_updatable; the corresponding PatchIn schema does NOT include these two fields, so Pydantic also rejects them at the request-validation layer.
decisions.rationale: The rare-correction case the spec's PATCH path was designed for is exactly the mis-ingestion scenario where the helper script (PI-030 work) captures the wrong parent SHAs from git log --format=%P, the wrong file-change count from git diff-tree --name-only -r <sha> | wc -l, or the wrong conversation attribution per methodology §5.6's between-conversations-manual-commit concern. Narrowing PATCH to a smaller field set would force soft-delete-and-recreate for those scenarios, which §3.4.3 explicitly characterizes as 'reserved for the case where the correction changes the entity identity' — and parent SHAs, file counts, and conversation attribution are NOT identity. The DB-level constraint that does protect identity (UNIQUE on commit_sha) holds regardless. The PATCH path remains the lightweight correction mechanism the spec intended; the soft-delete-and-recreate path remains reserved for the genuinely identity-changing case.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Narrow PATCH to the message and authorship fields only, treating parent_shas / files_changed_count / conversation_id as identity-like and requiring soft-delete-and-recreate for their correction. Rejected — those three fields capture observable git metadata or attribution metadata that can be wrong at ingestion (helper bugs, manual-commit attribution mistakes); forcing soft-delete-and-recreate for what is materially the same git object is overkill and conflicts with the spec's stated correction posture in §3.4.3. Lock PATCH to no fields, requiring all corrections to go through PUT. Rejected — the PUT path requires a full-record body, which is more friction than the rare-correction case warrants, and PUT does not change the identity-protection logic (commit_identifier and commit_sha are still rejected on PUT too).
decisions.consequences: The _PATCHABLE_FIELDS frozenset in commits.py admits ten fields. The CommitPatchIn Pydantic schema declares ten optional fields and rejects commit_identifier and commit_sha at the request-validation layer (extra='forbid' inherited from _Base). The administrative-correction test surface (DEC-212 tests in test_commit.py) covers patching parent_shas to a new value, patching files_changed_count to a new value, patching conversation_id with FK existence check, and the two rejection paths (commit_identifier and commit_sha patches return 422). Future workstreams correcting historical commit metadata use PATCH as the primary mechanism; the soft-delete-and-recreate path remains documented in the commit.md spec for the rare wrong-SHA case.

decisions.title: By-sha endpoint specifics: minimum prefix length 4, uppercase input lowercased before query, ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses 'candidates' field with full 40-char SHAs
decisions.context: commit.md §3.5.1 sketched the GET /commits/by-sha/{sha} endpoint as accepting a full 40-character SHA or a prefix of any length 4+, with four-case behavior (full hit, unambiguous prefix hit, ambiguous prefix → 409, miss → 404). Three sub-questions were left to build-planning: (a) minimum prefix length — confirm 4 hex characters is enough, or bump to 7 to match git's --short default; (b) case normalization — what does the endpoint do if the caller supplies uppercase or mixed case input, given the schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1; (c) ambiguous-prefix 409 response body shape — field name and whether candidates are full SHAs or identifiers or both.
decisions.decision: (a) Minimum prefix length is 4 hex characters per the spec's '4+' language. Prefixes of length 1-3 return HTTP 422 with code prefix_too_short. (b) Uppercase or mixed-case input is lowercased before the query rather than rejected with HTTP 422. The schema stores lowercase per §3.2.1; the endpoint transparently normalizes. The same lowercasing applies whether the input is a full 40-char SHA or a 4+ char prefix. (c) The ambiguous-prefix 409 response body uses the field name 'candidates' (per the spec's sketch in §3.5.1) carrying full 40-char SHAs only. The response body shape is {"detail": {"code": "ambiguous_sha_prefix", "candidates": ["<sha-1>", "<sha-2>", ...]}}; the candidates list contains every commit_sha value whose lowercase form starts with the lowercased input prefix (excluding soft-deleted unless ?include_deleted=true).
decisions.rationale: (a) Git's --short default is 7 but the spec settled on 4, and the conversation did not surface evidence to bump it. 4 is enough to disambiguate in this project's expected commit volume (hundreds, not the tens of thousands where 7-char prefixes become necessary). (b) Rejecting uppercase as HTTP 422 is a worse API ergonomic than transparently normalizing — git's own commands accept both cases, and the schema's lowercase storage is an internal canonicalization choice that should not leak into the API contract. (c) The 'candidates' field name comes directly from the spec's §3.5.1 sketch. Full 40-char SHAs match the entity's natural key (the caller is asking by SHA; returning SHAs is the symmetric response); V2 identifiers could be added to the response body in a future version if a UI use case wants them, but for v0.8 the bare SHA list is sufficient and minimizes payload size.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Bump minimum prefix length to 7 to match git's --short default. Rejected — the spec settled on 4 and the project's commit volume does not justify the tighter minimum. (b) Reject uppercase input with HTTP 422. Rejected — worse API ergonomic than normalizing. Strict-validation arguments favor rejection, but transparent normalization is the more common API convention for case-insensitive natural keys (git's own behavior, GitHub's SHA URLs, GitLab's SHA URLs). (c) Return V2 identifiers (CM-NNNN list) as candidates instead of or in addition to the SHAs. Rejected for v0.8 — the caller is asking by SHA so SHAs are the symmetric response; identifiers could be added later if a concrete UI use case wants them.
decisions.consequences: The access-layer's find_by_sha function returns three return shapes: a dict for unambiguous hits, a list[str] for ambiguous prefixes (router maps to 409), or None for misses (router maps to 404). The function lowercases its sha_or_prefix input parameter before query; the SQL startswith does NOT case-normalize, so the access-layer lowercase is the canonical normalization point. The router-level by-sha endpoint raises FastAPI HTTPException with status_code=409 and detail={code: 'ambiguous_sha_prefix', candidates: [...]} for ambiguous matches, and HTTPException with status_code=404 and detail={code: 'commit_sha_not_found', value: <input>} for misses. The test surface in test_commit.py and test_commit_api.py covers all four cases plus the prefix-too-short rejection plus the uppercase-normalization happy path plus the soft-delete exclusion behavior.

decisions.title: List endpoint default sort is commit_committed_at descending; sortable column set locked to a known-safe subset; ?sort and ?order query parameters per V2 list convention
decisions.context: commit.md §3.6.2 names the UI master pane's default sort as commit_committed_at descending — the operational pattern for 'what just happened' queries, parallel to deposit_event's deviation from the V2 default ascending sort per the audit-log nature of the entity. The kickoff's Q5 asked whether the list endpoint's default sort and the sort/order query parameter shape should match the V2 list-endpoint pattern. The decision determines the list endpoint's default sort, the accepted query parameter shape, and the safety posture for the sort column input.
decisions.decision: API default sort is commit_committed_at descending matching the UI master pane convention. The endpoint accepts ?sort=<column> and ?order=asc|desc per V2's list-endpoint pattern. The sortable column set is locked at the access layer to a known-safe subset: commit_identifier, commit_committed_at, commit_repository, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at. Invalid sort columns return HTTP 422 with code invalid_sort_column. Invalid order values (anything other than 'asc' or 'desc') return HTTP 422 with code invalid_order. The known-safe-subset approach prevents free-form column injection via the sort parameter while admitting every column an operational query would reasonably want to sort by.
decisions.rationale: Matching the UI master pane convention (commit_committed_at descending) keeps the API and UI surfaces consistent — an API consumer running a list query and pasting into the UI master pane gets identical ordering. The known-safe-subset approach is the V2 convention for sort columns; free-form column input is rejected by other list endpoints similarly. The five admitted columns cover the operational query patterns: by audit time (commit_committed_at, commit_created_at, commit_updated_at), by identity (commit_identifier), and by physical home (commit_repository). Columns NOT in the safe subset (commit_message_first_line, commit_message_full, commit_author_name, commit_author_email, commit_branch, commit_parent_shas, commit_files_changed_count, commit_conversation_id, commit_deleted_at) are excluded because they are unlikely to drive useful sort orders and because expanding the set after the fact is one additive change.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Match V2's apparent default ascending sort across other entity types. Rejected — for commits, descending matches the audit-log nature of the entity and the UI master pane convention; the operational pattern for 'what just happened' queries wants most-recent first. Admit any column for sort with free-form input. Rejected — opens the door to injection of unintended columns and makes column-rename refactors more dangerous; the known-safe subset is the standard V2 posture for sort parameter validation. Lock the sort to commit_committed_at only and disallow user-supplied sort parameters. Rejected — too restrictive for legitimate audit queries that want to sort by identity or by physical home (e.g., 'show me every commit in the crmbuilder repo by identity').
decisions.consequences: The access-layer's list_commits function accepts sort and order keyword arguments with defaults commit_committed_at and desc. Sort validation runs at function entry with the _SORTABLE_COLUMNS frozenset; order validation accepts only 'asc' or 'desc'. The router exposes both as query parameters with the same defaults. The test surface in test_commit.py covers the default-descending behavior, the asc reversal, the invalid-column rejection, and the invalid-order rejection. Future workstreams adding new sortable columns extend the _SORTABLE_COLUMNS set in one additive change; the API surface remains backward-compatible because previously-valid sort values continue to validate.
